Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name=Tagata Shrine |Image=[[File:Tagata jinja haiden 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537}} Tagata-chō 152 |Coordinates={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|57.03|N|136|56|28.19|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
Tamahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded=Unknown |Main hall={{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, copper-sheet roofing |Annual festival={{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403}} (Hōnen Festival) |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Tagata Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Tagata Shrine''' (たがたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It is a {{ill|lt=minor Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It is known for the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival}} held every March. The character “縣” (agata, ken) is the {{ill|lt=old character form|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=old form}} of “県” (and is sometimes considered a {{ill|lt=variant character|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=variant}}), so it is also written as '''Tagata Shrine''' (田県神社). === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (みとしのかみ) and Tamahime-no-Mikoto (たまひめのみこと), gods of abundant harvests and the prosperity of descendants. According to shrine tradition, this area was part of the estate of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oarata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, where Mitoshi-no-Kami, a god of bountiful crops, was worshiped. Tamahime-no-Mikoto was the daughter of Oarata-no-Mikoto; after her husband’s death, she returned to her parental home, raised her children admirably as a mother, and helped her father cultivate the land. In praise of these merits, she came to be enshrined as a deity. Within the shrine grounds, many stones shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=phallic symbols|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. === History === The date of foundation is unknown, and it is considered a very old shrine {{citation needed|date=September 2017}}. It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - panoramio.jpg|Torii gate File:Tagata jinja okumiya 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|Inner shrine File:Tagata Shrine 4.JPG|Prayer offerings File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 2.jpg|Chinpō Cave File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 1.jpg|Sacred object File:Phallus stone pole, Tagata Shrine.jpg|Stone pillar File:Phallus cake, Hōnen Matsuri (Tagata Shrine) 2.jpg|Chinbō San File:Hōnen Matsuri 2.JPG|Hōnen Festival File:Hōnen Matsuri 6.JPG|Hōnen Festival === Access === * About a 5-minute walk (approx. 200 m) from {{ill|lt=Tagata Shrine-mae Station|Q7468612|WD=Q7468612}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Komaki Line|Q6788786|WD=Q6788786|lt=Komaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu}}. === Related books === * Niwa Kinji (ed.), “Catalogue of Niwa Library Ancient Documents and Calligraphy Related to the Restoration of Tagata Shrine (Survey dated February 11, 1975),” 1975. * Nakai Fumio (author), “Making Japan’s Largest Wooden Phallus — Oral Accounts of Tagata Shrine Master Carpenter Saiki Kunimitsu,” Kameyama Iwao, 1981. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732}} — A shrine in (Kawasaki)|Q112074451|WD=Q112074451|lt=Kanayama Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Kawasaki City|Q164234|WD=Q164234}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}, known for the Kanamara Festival. * {{ill|lt=Dontsuku Shrine|Q131925949|WD=Q131925949}} — A shrine that enshrines a large wooden male organ similar to Tagata Shrine and holds the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dontsuku Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} using mikoshi and floats carrying it. * {{ill|lt=Marakanon|Q44393618|WD=Q44393618}} — A temple associated with the {{ill|lt=Ōuchi clan|Q1348909|WD=Q1348909}} in {{ill|lt=Nagato City|Q390812|WD=Q390812}}, {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|Q127264|WD=Q127264}}. === External links === * {{official|http://www.tagatajinja.com/}} * [https://www.city.komaki.aichi.jp/admin/event_1/midokoro/kankou/2/10628.html - panoramio.jpg {{commons category|Category:Tagata Shrine}} [[EN:Tagata Shrine]] [[FR:Tagata-jinja]] [[JA:田縣神社]] [[MIN:Kuil Tagata]] [[SIMPLE:Tagata Shrine]] [[ZH:田縣神社]] {{wikidata link|Q60581}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name=Tagata Shrine |Image=[[File:Tagata jinja haiden 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}{{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537}} Tagata-chō 152 |Coordinates={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|57.03|N|136|56|28.19|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
Tamahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded=Unknown |Main hall={{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, copper-sheet roofing |Annual festival={{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403}} (Hōnen Festival) |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Tagata Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Tagata Shrine''' (たがたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in {{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434}}. It is a {{ill|lt=minor Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It is known for the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival}} held every March. The character “縣” (agata, ken) is the {{ill|lt=old character form|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=old form}} of “県” (and is sometimes considered a {{ill|lt=variant character|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=variant}}), so it is also written as '''Tagata Shrine''' (田県神社). === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (みとしのかみ) and Tamahime-no-Mikoto (たまひめのみこと), gods of abundant harvests and the prosperity of descendants. According to shrine tradition, this area was part of the estate of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oarata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, where Mitoshi-no-Kami, a god of bountiful crops, was worshiped. Tamahime-no-Mikoto was the daughter of Oarata-no-Mikoto; after her husband’s death, she returned to her parental home, raised her children admirably as a mother, and helped her father cultivate the land. In praise of these merits, she came to be enshrined as a deity. Within the shrine grounds, many stones shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=phallic symbols|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. === History === The date of foundation is unknown, and it is considered a very old shrine {{citation needed|date=September 2017}}. It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - panoramio.jpg|Torii gate File:Tagata jinja okumiya 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|Inner shrine File:Tagata Shrine 4.JPG|Prayer offerings File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 2.jpg|Chinpō Cave File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 1.jpg|Sacred object File:Phallus stone pole, Tagata Shrine.jpg|Stone pillar File:Phallus cake, Hōnen Matsuri (Tagata Shrine) 2.jpg|Chinbō San File:Hōnen Matsuri 2.JPG|Hōnen Festival File:Hōnen Matsuri 6.JPG|Hōnen Festival === Access === * About a 5-minute walk (approx. 200 m) from {{ill|lt=Tagata Shrine-mae Station|Q7468612|WD=Q7468612}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Komaki Line|Q6788786|WD=Q6788786|lt=Komaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu}}. === Related books === * Niwa Kinji (ed.), “Catalogue of Niwa Library Ancient Documents and Calligraphy Related to the Restoration of Tagata Shrine (Survey dated February 11, 1975),” 1975. * Nakai Fumio (author), “Making Japan’s Largest Wooden Phallus — Oral Accounts of Tagata Shrine Master Carpenter Saiki Kunimitsu,” Kameyama Iwao, 1981. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732}} — A shrine in (Kawasaki)|Q112074451|WD=Q112074451|lt=Kanayama Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Kawasaki City|Q164234|WD=Q164234}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513}}, known for the Kanamara Festival. * {{ill|lt=Dontsuku Shrine|Q131925949|WD=Q131925949}} — A shrine that enshrines a large wooden male organ similar to Tagata Shrine and holds the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dontsuku Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} using mikoshi and floats carrying it. * {{ill|lt=Marakanon|Q44393618|WD=Q44393618}} — A temple associated with the {{ill|lt=Ōuchi clan|Q1348909|WD=Q1348909}} in {{ill|lt=Nagato City|Q390812|WD=Q390812}}, {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|Q127264|WD=Q127264}}. === External links === * {{official|http://www.tagatajinja.com/}} * [https://www.city.komaki.aichi.jp/admin/event_1/midokoro/kankou/2/10628.html link|Q60581}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name=Tagata Shrine |Image=[[File:Tagata jinja haiden 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}} Tagata-chō 152 |Coordinates={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|57.03|N|136|56|28.19|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
Tamahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded=Unknown |Main hall={{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-sheet roofing |Annual festival={{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}} (Hōnen Festival) |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Tagata Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Tagata Shrine''' (たがたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=minor Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is known for the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} held every March. The character “縣” (agata, ken) is the {{ill|lt=old character form|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=old form|1=Chinese character forms}} of “県” (and is sometimes considered a {{ill|lt=variant character|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=variant|1=Chinese character forms}}), so it is also written as '''Tagata Shrine''' (田県神社). === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (みとしのかみ) and Tamahime-no-Mikoto (たまひめのみこと), gods of abundant harvests and the prosperity of descendants. According to shrine tradition, this area was part of the estate of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oarata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, where Mitoshi-no-Kami, a god of bountiful crops, was worshiped. Tamahime-no-Mikoto was the daughter of Oarata-no-Mikoto; after her husband’s death, she returned to her parental home, raised her children admirably as a mother, and helped her father cultivate the land. In praise of these merits, she came to be enshrined as a deity. Within the shrine grounds, many stones shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=phallic symbols|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. === History === The date of foundation is unknown, and it is considered a very old shrine {{citation needed|date=September 2017}}. It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083|1=Male reproductive system}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451|1=Agriculture}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153|1=Jōji}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185|1=1364}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - panoramio.jpg|Torii gate File:Tagata jinja okumiya 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|Inner shrine File:Tagata Shrine 4.JPG|Prayer offerings File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 2.jpg|Chinpō Cave File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 1.jpg|Sacred object File:Phallus stone pole, Tagata Shrine.jpg|Stone pillar File:Phallus cake, Hōnen Matsuri (Tagata Shrine) 2.jpg|Chinbō San File:Hōnen Matsuri 2.JPG|Hōnen Festival File:Hōnen Matsuri 6.JPG|Hōnen Festival === Access === * About a 5-minute walk (approx. 200 m) from {{ill|lt=Tagata Shrine-mae Station|Q7468612|WD=Q7468612|1=Tagata Jinja-mae Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Komaki Line|Q6788786|WD=Q6788786|lt=Komaki Line|1=Meitetsu Komaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related books === * Niwa Kinji (ed.), “Catalogue of Niwa Library Ancient Documents and Calligraphy Related to the Restoration of Tagata Shrine (Survey dated February 11, 1975),” 1975. * Nakai Fumio (author), “Making Japan’s Largest Wooden Phallus — Oral Accounts of Tagata Shrine Master Carpenter Saiki Kunimitsu,” Kameyama Iwao, 1981. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, where stones shaped like the vulva are enshrined, in contrast to Tagata Shrine. * {{ill|lt=Kanayama Shrine (Kawasaki)|Q112074451|WD=Q112074451|lt=Kanayama Shrine|1=Kanayama Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Kawasaki City|Q164234|WD=Q164234|1=Kawasaki, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, known for the Kanamara Festival. * {{ill|lt=Dontsuku Shrine|Q131925949|WD=Q131925949|1=Dontsuku Shrine}} — A shrine that enshrines a large wooden male organ similar to Tagata Shrine and holds the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dontsuku Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} using mikoshi and floats carrying it. * {{ill|lt=Marakanon|Q44393618|WD=Q44393618|1=Mara Kannon Temple}} — A temple associated with the {{ill|lt=Ōuchi clan|Q1348909|WD=Q1348909|1=Ōuchi clan}} in {{ill|lt=Nagato City|Q390812|WD=Q390812|1=Nagato, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|Q127264|WD=Q127264|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}. === External links === * It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083|1=Male reproductive system}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451|1=Agriculture}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153|1=Jōji}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185|1=1364}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - panoramio.jpg|Torii gate File:Tagata jinja okumiya 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|Inner shrine File:Tagata Shrine 4.JPG|Prayer offerings File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 2.jpg|Chinpō Cave File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 1.jpg|Sacred object File:Phallus stone pole, Tagata Shrine.jpg|Stone pillar File:Phallus cake, Hōnen Matsuri (Tagata Shrine) 2.jpg|Chinbō San File:Hōnen Matsuri 2.JPG|Hōnen Festival File:Hōnen Matsuri 6.JPG|Hōnen Festival === Access === * About a 5-minute walk (approx. 200 m) from {{ill|lt=Tagata Shrine-mae Station|Q7468612|WD=Q7468612|1=Tagata Jinja-mae Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Komaki Line|Q6788786|WD=Q6788786|lt=Komaki Line|1=Meitetsu Komaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related books === * Niwa Kinji (ed.), “Catalogue of Niwa Library Ancient Documents and Calligraphy Related to the Restoration of Tagata Shrine (Survey dated February 11, 1975),” 1975. * Nakai Fumio (author), “Making Japan’s Largest Wooden Phallus — Oral Accounts of Tagata Shrine Master Carpenter Saiki Kunimitsu,” Kameyama Iwao, 1981. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, where stones shaped like the vulva are enshrined, in contrast to Tagata Shrine. * {{ill|lt=Kanayama Shrine (Kawasaki)|Q112074451|WD=Q112074451|lt=Kanayama Shrine|1=Kanayama Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Kawasaki City|Q164234|WD=Q164234|1=Kawasaki, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, known for the Kanamara Festival. * {{ill|lt=Dontsuku Shrine|Q131925949|WD=Q131925949|1=Dontsuku Shrine}} — A shrine that enshrines a large wooden male organ similar to Tagata Shrine and holds the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dontsuku Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} using mikoshi and floats carrying it. * {{ill|lt=Marakanon|Q44393618|WD=Q44393618|1=Mara Kannon Temple}} — A temple associated with the {{ill|lt=Ōuchi clan|Q1348909|WD=Q1348909|1=Ōuchi clan}} in {{ill|lt=Nagato City|Q390812|WD=Q390812|1=Nagato, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|Q127264|WD=Q127264|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}. === External links === * {{official|http://www.tagatajinja.com/}} * [https://www.city.komaki.aichi.jp/admin/event_1/midokoro/kankou/2/10628.html Hōnen Festival of Tagata Shrine] Komaki City {{Shinto Navbox}} {{Normdaten}} Haiden (worship hall) |Location={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}} Tagata-chō 152 |Coordinates={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|57.03|N|136|56|28.19|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
Tamahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded=Unknown |Main hall={{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-sheet roofing |Annual festival={{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}} (Hōnen Festival) |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Tagata Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Tagata Shrine''' (たがたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=minor Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is known for the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} held every March. The character “縣” (agata, ken) is the {{ill|lt=old character form|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=old form|1=Chinese character forms}} of “県” (and is sometimes considered a {{ill|lt=variant character|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=variant|1=Chinese character forms}}), so it is also written as '''Tagata Shrine''' (田県神社). === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (みとしのかみ) and Tamahime-no-Mikoto (たまひめのみこと), gods of abundant harvests and the prosperity of descendants. According to shrine tradition, this area was part of the estate of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oarata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, where Mitoshi-no-Kami, a god of bountiful crops, was worshiped. Tamahime-no-Mikoto was the daughter of Oarata-no-Mikoto; after her husband’s death, she returned to her parental home, raised her children admirably as a mother, and helped her father cultivate the land. In praise of these merits, she came to be enshrined as a deity. Within the shrine grounds, many stones shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=phallic symbols|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. === History === The date of foundation is unknown, and it is considered a very old shrine {{citation needed|date=September 2017}}. It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083|1=Male reproductive system}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451|1=Agriculture}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153|1=Jōji}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185|1=1364}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - Jinja-mae Station|id|Stasiun Tagata Jinja-mae|ja|田県神社前駅|jv|Setatsiyun Tagata Jinja-mae|ko|다가타진자마에역|zh|田县神社前站|WD=Q7468612|1=Tagata Jinja-mae Station}} == image of entrance (P9721) == * Tagata Jinja Shrine,田縣神社 - panoramio.jpg {{commons category|Category:Tagata Shrine}} [[EN:Tagata Shrine]] [[FR:Tagata-jinja]] [[JA:田縣神社]] [[MIN:Kuil Tagata]] [[SIMPLE:Tagata Shrine]] [[ZH:田縣神社]] {{wikidata link|Q60581}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name=Tagata Shrine |Image=[[File:Tagata jinja haiden 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}} Tagata-chō 152 |Coordinates={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|57.03|N|136|56|28.19|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
Tamahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded=Unknown |Main hall={{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-sheet roofing |Annual festival={{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}} (Hōnen Festival) |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Tagata Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Tagata Shrine''' (たがたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=minor Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is known for the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} held every March. The character “縣” (agata, ken) is the {{ill|lt=old character form|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=old form|1=Chinese character forms}} of “県” (and is sometimes considered a {{ill|lt=variant character|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=variant|1=Chinese character forms}}), so it is also written as '''Tagata Shrine''' (田県神社). === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (みとしのかみ) and Tamahime-no-Mikoto (たまひめのみこと), gods of abundant harvests and the prosperity of descendants. According to shrine tradition, this area was part of the estate of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oarata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, where Mitoshi-no-Kami, a god of bountiful crops, was worshiped. Tamahime-no-Mikoto was the daughter of Oarata-no-Mikoto; after her husband’s death, she returned to her parental home, raised her children admirably as a mother, and helped her father cultivate the land. In praise of these merits, she came to be enshrined as a deity. Within the shrine grounds, many stones shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=phallic symbols|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. === History === The date of foundation is unknown, and it is considered a very old shrine {{citation needed|date=September 2017}}. It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083|1=Male reproductive system}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451|1=Agriculture}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153|1=Jōji}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185|1=1364}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - panoramio.jpg|Torii gate File:Tagata jinja okumiya 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|Inner shrine File:Tagata Shrine 4.JPG|Prayer offerings File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 2.jpg|Chinpō Cave File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 1.jpg|Sacred object File:Phallus stone pole, Tagata Shrine.jpg|Stone pillar File:Phallus cake, Hōnen Matsuri (Tagata Shrine) 2.jpg|Chinbō San File:Hōnen Matsuri 2.JPG|Hōnen Festival File:Hōnen Matsuri 6.JPG|Hōnen Festival === Access === * About a 5-minute walk (approx. 200 m) from {{ill|lt=Tagata Shrine-mae Station|Q7468612|WD=Q7468612|1=Tagata Jinja-mae Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Komaki Line|Q6788786|WD=Q6788786|lt=Komaki Line|1=Meitetsu Komaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related books === * Niwa Kinji (ed.), “Catalogue of Niwa Library Ancient Documents and Calligraphy Related to the Restoration of Tagata Shrine (Survey dated February 11, 1975),” 1975. * Nakai Fumio (author), “Making Japan’s Largest Wooden Phallus — Oral Accounts of Tagata Shrine Master Carpenter Saiki Kunimitsu,” Kameyama Iwao, 1981. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, where stones shaped like the vulva are enshrined, in contrast to Tagata Shrine. * {{ill|lt=Kanayama Shrine (Kawasaki)|Q112074451|WD=Q112074451|lt=Kanayama Shrine|1=Kanayama Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Kawasaki City|Q164234|WD=Q164234|1=Kawasaki, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, known for the Kanamara Festival. * {{ill|lt=Dontsuku Shrine|Q131925949|WD=Q131925949|1=Dontsuku Shrine}} — A shrine that enshrines a large wooden male organ similar to Tagata Shrine and holds the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dontsuku Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} using mikoshi and floats carrying it. * {{ill|lt=Marakanon|Q44393618|WD=Q44393618|1=Mara Kannon Temple}} — A temple associated with the {{ill|lt=Ōuchi clan|Q1348909|WD=Q1348909|1=Ōuchi clan}} in {{ill|lt=Nagato City|Q390812|WD=Q390812|1=Nagato, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|Q127264|WD=Q127264|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}. === External links === * {{official|http://www.tagatajinja.com/}} * [https://www.city.komaki.aichi.jp/admin/event_1/midokoro/kankou/2/10628.html Hōnen Festival of Tagata Shrine] Komaki City {{Shinto Navbox}} {{Normdaten}} Shrine,田縣神社 - panoramio.jpg {{commons category|Category:Tagata Shrine}} [[EN:Tagata Shrine]] [[FR:Tagata-jinja]] [[JA:田縣神社]] [[MIN:Kuil Tagata]] [[SIMPLE:Tagata Shrine]] [[ZH:田縣神社]] {{wikidata link|Q60581}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name=Tagata Shrine |Image=[[File:Tagata jinja haiden 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}} Tagata-chō 152 |Coordinates={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|57.03|N|136|56|28.19|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
Tamahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded=Unknown |Main hall={{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-sheet roofing |Annual festival={{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}} (Hōnen Festival) |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Tagata Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Tagata Shrine''' (たがたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=minor Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is known for the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} held every March. The character “縣” (agata, ken) is the {{ill|lt=old character form|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=old form|1=Chinese character forms}} of “県” (and is sometimes considered a {{ill|lt=variant character|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=variant|1=Chinese character forms}}), so it is also written as '''Tagata Shrine''' (田県神社). === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (みとしのかみ) and Tamahime-no-Mikoto (たまひめのみこと), gods of abundant harvests and the prosperity of descendants. According to shrine tradition, this area was part of the estate of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oarata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, where Mitoshi-no-Kami, a god of bountiful crops, was worshiped. Tamahime-no-Mikoto was the daughter of Oarata-no-Mikoto; after her husband’s death, she returned to her parental home, raised her children admirably as a mother, and helped her father cultivate the land. In praise of these merits, she came to be enshrined as a deity. Within the shrine grounds, many stones shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=phallic symbols|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. === History === The date of foundation is unknown, and it is considered a very old shrine {{citation needed|date=September 2017}}. It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083|1=Male reproductive system}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451|1=Agriculture}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153|1=Jōji}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185|1=1364}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - panoramio.jpg|Torii gate File:Tagata jinja okumiya 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|Inner shrine File:Tagata Shrine 4.JPG|Prayer offerings File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 2.jpg|Chinpō Cave File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 1.jpg|Sacred object File:Phallus stone pole, Tagata Shrine.jpg|Stone pillar File:Phallus cake, Hōnen Matsuri (Tagata Shrine) 2.jpg|Chinbō San File:Hōnen Matsuri 2.JPG|Hōnen Festival File:Hōnen Matsuri 6.JPG|Hōnen Festival === Access === * About a 5-minute walk (approx. 200 m) from {{ill|lt=Tagata Shrine-mae Station|Q7468612|WD=Q7468612|1=Tagata Jinja-mae Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Komaki Line|Q6788786|WD=Q6788786|lt=Komaki Line|1=Meitetsu Komaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related books === * Niwa Kinji (ed.), “Catalogue of Niwa Library Ancient Documents and Calligraphy Related to the Restoration of Tagata Shrine (Survey dated February 11, 1975),” 1975. * Nakai Fumio (author), “Making Japan’s Largest Wooden Phallus — Oral Accounts of Tagata Shrine Master Carpenter Saiki Kunimitsu,” Kameyama Iwao, 1981. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, where stones shaped like the vulva are enshrined, in contrast to Tagata Shrine. * {{ill|lt=Kanayama Shrine (Kawasaki)|Q112074451|WD=Q112074451|lt=Kanayama Shrine|1=Kanayama Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Kawasaki City|Q164234|WD=Q164234|1=Kawasaki, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, known for the Kanamara Festival. * {{ill|lt=Dontsuku Shrine|Q131925949|WD=Q131925949|1=Dontsuku Shrine}} — A shrine that enshrines a large wooden male organ similar to Tagata Shrine and holds the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dontsuku Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} using mikoshi and floats carrying it. * {{ill|lt=Marakanon|Q44393618|WD=Q44393618|1=Mara Kannon Temple}} — A temple associated with the {{ill|lt=Ōuchi clan|Q1348909|WD=Q1348909|1=Ōuchi clan}} in {{ill|lt=Nagato City|Q390812|WD=Q390812|1=Nagato, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|Q127264|WD=Q127264|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}. === External links === * {{official|http://www.tagatajinja.com/}} * [https://www.city.komaki.aichi.jp/admin/event_1/midokoro/kankou/2/10628.html Hōnen Festival of Tagata Shrine] Komaki City {{Shinto Navbox}} {{Normdaten}} {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Tagata Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Tagata Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:History of Komaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Komaki]] [[Category:尾張国の式内社]] Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - panoramio.jpg|Torii gate File:Tagata jinja okumiya 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|Inner shrine File:Tagata Shrine 4.JPG|Prayer offerings File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 2.jpg|Chinpō Cave File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 1.jpg|Sacred object File:Phallus stone pole, Tagata Shrine.jpg|Stone pillar File:Phallus cake, Hōnen Matsuri (Tagata Shrine) 2.jpg|Chinbō San File:Hōnen Matsuri 2.JPG|Hōnen Festival File:Hōnen Matsuri 6.JPG|Hōnen Festival === Access === * About a 5-minute walk (approx. 200 m) from {{ill|lt=Tagata Shrine-mae Station|Q7468612|WD=Q7468612|1=Tagata Jinja-mae Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Komaki Line|Q6788786|WD=Q6788786|lt=Komaki Line|1=Meitetsu Komaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related books === * Niwa Kinji (ed.), “Catalogue of Niwa Library Ancient Documents and Calligraphy Related to the Restoration of Tagata Shrine (Survey dated February 11, 1975),” 1975. * Nakai Fumio (author), “Making Japan’s Largest Wooden Phallus — Oral Accounts of Tagata Shrine Master Carpenter Saiki Kunimitsu,” Kameyama Iwao, 1981. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, where stones shaped like the vulva are enshrined, in contrast to Tagata Shrine. * {{ill|lt=Kanayama Shrine (Kawasaki)|Q112074451|WD=Q112074451|lt=Kanayama Shrine|1=Kanayama Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Kawasaki City|Q164234|WD=Q164234|1=Kawasaki, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, known for the Kanamara Festival. * {{ill|lt=Dontsuku Shrine|Q131925949|WD=Q131925949|1=Dontsuku Shrine}} — A shrine that enshrines a large wooden male organ similar to Tagata Shrine and holds the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dontsuku Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} using mikoshi and floats carrying it. * {{ill|lt=Marakanon|Q44393618|WD=Q44393618|1=Mara Kannon Temple}} — A temple associated with the {{ill|lt=Ōuchi clan|Q1348909|WD=Q1348909|1=Ōuchi clan}} in {{ill|lt=Nagato City|Q390812|WD=Q390812|1=Nagato, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|Q127264|WD=Q127264|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}. === External links === * Haiden (worship hall) |Location={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}} Tagata-chō 152 |Coordinates={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|57.03|N|136|56|28.19|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
Tamahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded=Unknown |Main hall={{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-sheet roofing |Annual festival={{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}} (Hōnen Festival) |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Tagata Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Tagata Shrine''' (たがたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=minor Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is known for the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} held every March. The character “縣” (agata, ken) is the {{ill|lt=old character form|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=old form|1=Chinese character forms}} of “県” (and is sometimes considered a {{ill|lt=variant character|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=variant|1=Chinese character forms}}), so it is also written as '''Tagata Shrine''' (田県神社). === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (みとしのかみ) and Tamahime-no-Mikoto (たまひめのみこと), gods of abundant harvests and the prosperity of descendants. According to shrine tradition, this area was part of the estate of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oarata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, where Mitoshi-no-Kami, a god of bountiful crops, was worshiped. Tamahime-no-Mikoto was the daughter of Oarata-no-Mikoto; after her husband’s death, she returned to her parental home, raised her children admirably as a mother, and helped her father cultivate the land. In praise of these merits, she came to be enshrined as a deity. Within the shrine grounds, many stones shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=phallic symbols|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. === History === The date of foundation is unknown, and it is considered a very old shrine {{citation needed|date=September 2017}}. It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083|1=Male reproductive system}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451|1=Agriculture}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153|1=Jōji}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185|1=1364}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - Shrine,田縣神社 - panoramio.jpg {{commons category|Category:Tagata Shrine}} [[EN:Tagata Shrine]] [[FR:Tagata-jinja]] [[JA:田縣神社]] [[MIN:Kuil Tagata]] [[SIMPLE:Tagata Shrine]] [[ZH:田縣神社]] {{wikidata link|Q60581}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name=Tagata Shrine |Image=[[File:Tagata jinja haiden 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}} Tagata-chō 152 |Coordinates={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|57.03|N|136|56|28.19|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
Tamahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded=Unknown |Main hall={{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-sheet roofing |Annual festival={{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}} (Hōnen Festival) |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Tagata Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Tagata Shrine''' (たがたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=minor Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is known for the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} held every March. The character “縣” (agata, ken) is the {{ill|lt=old character form|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=old form|1=Chinese character forms}} of “県” (and is sometimes considered a {{ill|lt=variant character|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=variant|1=Chinese character forms}}), so it is also written as '''Tagata Shrine''' (田県神社). === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (みとしのかみ) and Tamahime-no-Mikoto (たまひめのみこと), gods of abundant harvests and the prosperity of descendants. According to shrine tradition, this area was part of the estate of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oarata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, where Mitoshi-no-Kami, a god of bountiful crops, was worshiped. Tamahime-no-Mikoto was the daughter of Oarata-no-Mikoto; after her husband’s death, she returned to her parental home, raised her children admirably as a mother, and helped her father cultivate the land. In praise of these merits, she came to be enshrined as a deity. Within the shrine grounds, many stones shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=phallic symbols|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. === History === The date of foundation is unknown, and it is considered a very old shrine {{citation needed|date=September 2017}}. It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083|1=Male reproductive system}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451|1=Agriculture}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153|1=Jōji}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185|1=1364}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - panoramio.jpg|Torii gate File:Tagata jinja okumiya 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|Inner shrine File:Tagata Shrine 4.JPG|Prayer offerings File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 2.jpg|Chinpō Cave File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 1.jpg|Sacred object File:Phallus stone pole, Tagata Shrine.jpg|Stone pillar File:Phallus cake, Hōnen Matsuri (Tagata Shrine) 2.jpg|Chinbō San File:Hōnen Matsuri 2.JPG|Hōnen Festival File:Hōnen Matsuri 6.JPG|Hōnen Festival === Access === * About a 5-minute walk (approx. 200 m) from {{ill|lt=Tagata Shrine-mae Station|Q7468612|WD=Q7468612|1=Tagata Jinja-mae Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Komaki Line|Q6788786|WD=Q6788786|lt=Komaki Line|1=Meitetsu Komaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related books === * Niwa Kinji (ed.), “Catalogue of Niwa Library Ancient Documents and Calligraphy Related to the Restoration of Tagata Shrine (Survey dated February 11, 1975),” 1975. * Nakai Fumio (author), “Making Japan’s Largest Wooden Phallus — Oral Accounts of Tagata Shrine Master Carpenter Saiki Kunimitsu,” Kameyama Iwao, 1981. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, where stones shaped like the vulva are enshrined, in contrast to Tagata Shrine. * {{ill|lt=Kanayama Shrine (Kawasaki)|Q112074451|WD=Q112074451|lt=Kanayama Shrine|1=Kanayama Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Kawasaki City|Q164234|WD=Q164234|1=Kawasaki, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, known for the Kanamara Festival. * {{ill|lt=Dontsuku Shrine|Q131925949|WD=Q131925949|1=Dontsuku Shrine}} — A shrine that enshrines a large wooden male organ similar to Tagata Shrine and holds the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dontsuku Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} using mikoshi and floats carrying it. * {{ill|lt=Marakanon|Q44393618|WD=Q44393618|1=Mara Kannon Temple}} — A temple associated with the {{ill|lt=Ōuchi clan|Q1348909|WD=Q1348909|1=Ōuchi clan}} in {{ill|lt=Nagato City|Q390812|WD=Q390812|1=Nagato, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|Q127264|WD=Q127264|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}. === External links === * {{official|http://www.tagatajinja.com/}} * [https://www.city.komaki.aichi.jp/admin/event_1/midokoro/kankou/2/10628.html Hōnen Festival of Tagata Shrine] Komaki City {{Shinto Navbox}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛知県の神社]] [[Category:History of Komaki]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Komaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Owari Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Aichi Prefecture]] [[Category:神道と性]] [[Category:Human gender and sexuality symbols]]

Shrine,田縣神社 - panoramio.jpg {{commons category|Category:Tagata Shrine}} [[EN:Tagata Shrine]] [[FR:Tagata-jinja]] [[JA:田縣神社]] [[MIN:Kuil Tagata]] [[SIMPLE:Tagata Shrine]] [[ZH:田縣神社]] {{wikidata link|Q60581}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name=Tagata Shrine |Image=[[File:Tagata jinja haiden 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location={{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}} Tagata-chō 152 |Coordinates={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|18|57.03|N|136|56|28.19|E|region:JP-23_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
Tamahime-no-Mikoto |Shrine rank={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |Founded=Unknown |Main hall={{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}, copper-sheet roofing |Annual festival={{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}} (Hōnen Festival) |Rituals= |Map= {{Location map|Japan Aichi|label=Tagata Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Tagata Shrine''' (たがたじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Komaki City|Q389537|WD=Q389537|1=Komaki}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=minor Shikinaisha|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}}, and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It is known for the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} held every March. The character “縣” (agata, ken) is the {{ill|lt=old character form|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=old form|1=Chinese character forms}} of “県” (and is sometimes considered a {{ill|lt=variant character|Q137673972|WD=Q137673972|lt=variant|1=Chinese character forms}}), so it is also written as '''Tagata Shrine''' (田県神社). === Enshrined deities === The enshrined deities are {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mitoshi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (みとしのかみ) and Tamahime-no-Mikoto (たまひめのみこと), gods of abundant harvests and the prosperity of descendants. According to shrine tradition, this area was part of the estate of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Oarata-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}, where Mitoshi-no-Kami, a god of bountiful crops, was worshiped. Tamahime-no-Mikoto was the daughter of Oarata-no-Mikoto; after her husband’s death, she returned to her parental home, raised her children admirably as a mother, and helped her father cultivate the land. In praise of these merits, she came to be enshrined as a deity. Within the shrine grounds, many stones shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=phallic symbols|1=UNKNOWN}} are enshrined. === History === The date of foundation is unknown, and it is considered a very old shrine {{citation needed|date=September 2017}}. It is a shrine based on ancient indigenous beliefs and is associated with phallic worship believed to improve {{ill|lt=male genital|Q842083|WD=Q842083|1=Male reproductive system}} fortune, linking faith in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=fertility|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=agriculture|Q11451|WD=Q11451|1=Agriculture}}. It is identified with “Tagata Shrine of Niwa District, {{ill|lt=Owari Province|Q514506|WD=Q514506|1=Owari Province}}” listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and with “Tagata Tenjin, Senior Third Rank” recorded in the *Owari Kokunai Jinmyōchō* from the third year of {{ill|lt=Jōji|Q1146153|WD=Q1146153|1=Jōji}} ({{ill|lt=1364|Q6185|WD=Q6185|1=1364}}). Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) - panoramio.jpg|Torii gate File:Tagata jinja okumiya 2020-12 ac (1).jpg|Inner shrine File:Tagata Shrine 4.JPG|Prayer offerings File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 2.jpg|Chinpō Cave File:Ensured Phallus object, Tagata Shrine 1.jpg|Sacred object File:Phallus stone pole, Tagata Shrine.jpg|Stone pillar File:Phallus cake, Hōnen Matsuri (Tagata Shrine) 2.jpg|Chinbō San File:Hōnen Matsuri 2.JPG|Hōnen Festival File:Hōnen Matsuri 6.JPG|Hōnen Festival === Access === * About a 5-minute walk (approx. 200 m) from {{ill|lt=Tagata Shrine-mae Station|Q7468612|WD=Q7468612|1=Tagata Jinja-mae Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Meitetsu Komaki Line|Q6788786|WD=Q6788786|lt=Komaki Line|1=Meitetsu Komaki Line}} of {{ill|lt=Nagoya Railroad|Q30850|WD=Q30850|lt=Meitetsu|1=Meitetsu}}. === Related books === * Niwa Kinji (ed.), “Catalogue of Niwa Library Ancient Documents and Calligraphy Related to the Restoration of Tagata Shrine (Survey dated February 11, 1975),” 1975. * Nakai Fumio (author), “Making Japan’s Largest Wooden Phallus — Oral Accounts of Tagata Shrine Master Carpenter Saiki Kunimitsu,” Kameyama Iwao, 1981. === Notes === {{Reflist}} === See also === {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}, {{ill|lt=Aichi Prefecture|Q80434|WD=Q80434|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, where stones shaped like the vulva are enshrined, in contrast to Tagata Shrine. * {{ill|lt=Kanayama Shrine (Kawasaki)|Q112074451|WD=Q112074451|lt=Kanayama Shrine|1=Kanayama Shrine}} — A shrine in {{ill|lt=Kawasaki City|Q164234|WD=Q164234|1=Kawasaki, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}, known for the Kanamara Festival. * {{ill|lt=Dontsuku Shrine|Q131925949|WD=Q131925949|1=Dontsuku Shrine}} — A shrine that enshrines a large wooden male organ similar to Tagata Shrine and holds the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Dontsuku Festival|1=UNKNOWN}} using mikoshi and floats carrying it. * {{ill|lt=Marakanon|Q44393618|WD=Q44393618|1=Mara Kannon Temple}} — A temple associated with the {{ill|lt=Ōuchi clan|Q1348909|WD=Q1348909|1=Ōuchi clan}} in {{ill|lt=Nagato City|Q390812|WD=Q390812|1=Nagato, Yamaguchi}}, {{ill|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|Q127264|WD=Q127264|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}. === External links === * {{official|http://www.tagatajinja.com/}} * [https://www.city.komaki.aichi.jp/admin/event_1/midokoro/kankou/2/10628.html Hōnen Festival of Tagata Shrine] Komaki City {{Shinto Navbox}} {{Normdaten}} {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Tagata Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Tagata Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] Since the current location was formerly in {{ill|lt=Kasugai District|Q11513576|WD=Q11513576|1=Kasugai district}}, the shrine must have been relocated at a later time. === Hōnen Festival === {{main|Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)}} Every year on {{ill|lt=March 15|Q2403|WD=Q2403|1=March 15}}, the {{ill|lt=Hōnen Festival (Tagata Shrine)|Q30924149|WD=Q30924149|lt=Hōnen Festival|1=Hōnensai}} is held. It is designated as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property of Komaki City and is read as “Hōnensai” or “Hōnenmatsuri,” also known as the “Henoko Festival.” Men parade while carrying a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940|1=Mikoshi}} bearing a large phallus called Ōowase-gata, while {{ill|lt=miko|Q1047139|WD=Q1047139|1=Miko}} carry smaller phallic objects. There is a tradition that touching them grants fertility. The Ōowase-gata measures about 60 cm in diameter and over 2 meters in length, and a new one is made each year from {{ill|lt=Japanese cypress|Q917187|WD=Q917187|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}} and dedicated to the shrine. This ritual symbolizes the phallus as “heaven” and the vulva as “earth,” praying that “through blessings from heaven, the land is nourished, resulting in abundant harvests and fertility.” The reason it is held in spring is that it also signifies “the birth of new life.” Nearby, the Hōnen Festival of {{ill|lt=Ōagata Shrine|Q135732|WD=Q135732|1=Ōagata Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Inuyama City|Q503411|WD=Q503411|1=Inuyama, Aichi}}—also known as the “Ososo Festival”—forms a pair with this festival; there, floats shaped like {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=vulva|1=UNKNOWN}} are paraded. === Characteristics === As souvenirs, candies, chocolates, and {{ill|lt=objects|Q7075109|WD=Q7075109|1=Objet d'art}} shaped like phallic symbols are sold. In recent years, it has become extremely popular with foreigners, and many foreign tourists visit. Only on the day of the Hōnen Festival, multiple {{ill|lt=parking lots|Q6501349|WD=Q6501349|1=Parking lot}} are set up near the shrine. === Gallery === File:Tagata Shrine Festival 20090315 (田縣神社の豊年祭) -
